segments.s197-g105: همیشه ('always') is an adverb that scopes over the whole wish; it precedes the noun phrase here for emphasis. بزی ('she‑goat') is the head noun; in classical/poetic Persian adjectives commonly follow the noun, so شاد ('merry') and برترمنش ('haughty/proud') come after it and are linked by و ('and'). برترمنش is a compound adjective (برتر + منش) meaning proud or haughty and functions like an adjectival epithet modifying بزی.
segments.s197-g106: ز is the poetic/pre‑classical form of از ('from'); the pattern «[subject] ز تو دور بادا» means 'may [subject] be far from you' with بادا as an optative particle ('may'). دور is the predicate adjective 'distant/far'. The final phrase بد بدکنش is a vocative/appositive insult addressing the addressee: بد ('wicked/bad') modifies بدکنش ('evildoer' or 'ill‑doer'), functioning as a condemnatory epithet tacked onto the wish.
segments.s197-g25: چنین گفت functions as a reporting phrase: چنین = 'thus' (sentence adverb) + گفت = 'said' (past tense reporting verb). کامد پلاشان شیر follows with inverted, poetic word order: کامد (archaic/past-form 'came' / 'has come') heads the clause; پلاشان modifies شیر (either adjectival 'tattered' or a noun 'cloak' attached to شیر), so پلاشان شیر = 'the lion of the tattered cloak' or 'lion in a tattered cloak'. The verb (کامد) appears before its noun phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s197-g26: یکى نامدارى سواری دلیر is a noun phrase giving identification: یکی = 'a certain' (indefinite determiner), نامداری used adjectivally = 'renowned', سواری = 'rider/horseman' (head noun), دلیر = 'brave' (adjective after the noun, normal in Persian poetic order). The whole phrase describes the arriving figure.
segments.s197-g27: شوم‌گر begins a conventional curse/conditional formula: شوم‌گر = 'may I be ill-fated/accursed if…' (literally 'woe/bad befall me if'). سرش را ببرم ز تن is a subjunctive/conditional clause: سرش = 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش = 'his'); را = object marker; ببرم = 1st-person singular present-subjunctive (classical/pers poetic form of 'to cut off' here translated 'I cut off'); ز تن = 'from the body'. Together it means 'may I be accursed if I cut off his head from his body.'
segments.s197-g28: گرش بسته آرم بدین انجمن is a conditional + result clause: گرش = contraction of گر + ش (گر = 'if', ش = 'him'); بسته = past participle/adjective 'bound, tied'; آرم is an archaic/poetic 1st-person verb form meaning 'I will bring' (here equivalent to آورم in later Persian) or 'I will set/lead'; بدین انجمن = 'to this assembly' (ب = 'to/into', دین here functions as the demonstrative 'this' in classical usage, and انجمن = 'assembly/meeting'). Literal: 'If I bind him, I will bring him to this assembly.'
segments.s197-g29: بدو گفت بیژن = 'to him Bijan said' with classical word order: بدو = 'to him/unto him' (indirect object prepositional form), گفت = 'said', بیژن = speaker's name (subject). که‌گر begins a subordinate conditional: که‌گر (که + گر / fused) = 'if' (introducing the conditional clause that follows), شهریار = 'the king' (subject of the conditional).
segments.s197-g30: مرا داد خلعت بدین کارزار is the remainder of the conditional clause: مرا = 'to/for me' (accusative/enclitic object), داد = past tense 'gave' (verb), خلعت = 'robe of honour' (direct object of داد), بدین = 'in/for this' (ب + این classicalized as بدین), کارزار = 'battle/war'. Word order is object-before-verb in this compresssed poetic line but reads 'if the king gives me a robe of honour in this battle...'.
segments.s197-g31: بفرمان مرا بست باید کمر: بفرمان = 'by the order/command (of)', مرا = 'me' or 'for me' (here marks the person affected), بست = verbal root 'to bind' (past/stative/infinitival form used with بایَد to indicate the action to be done), باید = 'must/it is necessary', کمر = 'waist/girdle' (object: 'gird the waist'). The whole phrase reads idiomatically as 'by my command (or at my command) I must gird my waist' (i.e., prepare for action).
segments.s197-g32: برزم پلاشان پرخاشخر strings together battlefield/combative notion and the opponent description: برزم here functions idiomatically as 'in the fight' or 'I must go forth to battle' (poetic/archaic verbal/locative use). پلاشان = 'the tattered/ragged ones' or 'those in tattered cloaks' (adjectival/noun phrase), پرخاشخر = 'quarrelsome, aggressive ones' (descriptive noun). Together: 'I must engage in battle with the tattered, quarrelsome folk.'
segments.s197-g41: Word order is possessive phrase + indirect object + imperative + purpose: سلیح سیاوش ("Siavash's weapon") is fronted, مرا is the recipient ('to me'), ده is the old/poetic imperative of دادن ('give!'), and بجنگ is an infinitival/purpose phrase meaning 'for fighting' or 'to use in battle'. The clause is an instruction: 'Give me Siavash's weapon to fight.'
segments.s197-g42: پس آنگه is a temporal sequence 'then / afterwards'; نگه کن is an imperative composed of the noun نگه ('watch/keep') + the imperative verb کن ('do!') meaning 'keep watch' or 'stand ready'; شکار پلنگ is the object phrase 'the leopard's quarry' or 'the leopard as quarry' and follows the verb. The whole segment is a follow-up command: 'then watch the leopard's prey.'
segments.s197-g43: بدو is the dative/enclitic meaning 'to him' (not the verb 'run'); داد is the past tense 'gave'; گیو دلیر is the giver's name plus epithet 'Giv the brave'; آن زره is the direct object 'that armor'. The clause literally: 'Giv the brave gave him that armor.'
segments.s197-g44: همی is a frequentive/continuous marker in epic Persian (here giving an ongoing or immediate past nuance); بست is the past form of بند(ن)/'to tie, fasten'; بیژن is the subject 'Bijan'; زره را uses را as the definite/object marker attached after the noun; گره is the direct object 'knot/strap'. The clause means 'Bijan then fastened the armor's knot/straps.'
segments.s197-g45: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'all at once / suddenly'; تیزرو ('swift one' or 'swift-rider') functions as the subject; برنشست is a past tense verb meaning 'rose up / mounted / sprang up'. The clause describes a sudden action: 'Suddenly the swift one sprang up.'
segments.s197-g46: بهامون is a dialectal/poetic contraction meaning 'toward us / to us'; خرامید is a past tense verb 'he strode/pranced' (a stately gait); نیزه بدست is an ablative/absolute phrase 'spear in hand' (بدست = 'in the hand'). Together: 'he strode toward us, spear in hand.'
segments.s197-g47: پلاشان here refers to the coarse/felt cloak (noun, 'cloak'), followed by یکی آهو 'a certain/a single gazelle'; افگنده بود is past perfective 'had cast/laid (down)'. The order is locative/possessive + object + verb: 'He had laid a gazelle on his cloak.'
segments.s197-g48: کبابش is 'its kebab/roast' with the enclitic -ش ('its'); بر آتش is the locative 'upon the fire'; پراگنده بود is 'was scattered/spread' (past). The clause says the gazelle's meat was laid out/cooking over the fire: 'its kebab was spread upon the fire.'
segments.s197-g1: سه روزش = 'three days' (سه روز) with the enclitic -ش referring back to the previously implied agent (they/them); the suffix here marks a possessive/relational link that yields a duration tied to that agent. درنگ آمد is a nominal/impersonal phrase 'a pause/delay came' (درنگ = pause, آمد = came) used to mean 'they tarried' — Persian often uses 'X آمد' to express that X happened. اندر چرم is a prepositional phrase ('in/within the hides') where اندر is a poetic equivalent of modern در.
segments.s197-g2: چهارم is an ordinal adverbial 'on the fourth (day)'; برآمد is past tense 'arose/issued forth' (literal 'rose up'). ز + شیپور indicates source ('from the trumpet'), with ز a poetic preposition for 'from'. دم here = 'blast/sound' (breath) produced by the trumpet; the phrase order is typical: temporal (چهارم) + verb (برآمد) + source phrase (ز شیپور دم).
segments.s197-g3: سپه برگرفت is a verbal phrase where سپه (host/army) is the subject and برگرفت is past-tense 'took up/raised' (literally 'took up'). و links the two coordinated actions. بزد is past 'struck/struck at' and governs the two instruments نای و کوس ('the ney and the war-drum') as its objects; note classical Persian freely omits an explicit object marker here.
segments.s197-g4: زمین کوه تا کوه گشت آبنوس = a verbless/resultative clause where زمین 'the land/ground' followed by the span 'کوه تا کوه' ('from mountain to mountain', تا used here as 'as far as/throughout') takes the verb گشت 'became/turned' and the predicative آبنوس 'ebony(black)'. Word order is subject + adverbial span + verb + predicate, a normal classical Persian arrangement for a resultant state.
segments.s197-g5: هرآنکس که دیدی is a relative clause 'whoever/whomever you saw' (هرآنکس = whoever; که introduces the clause; دیدی is 2nd-person past 'you saw'). ز توران سپاه is a genitive/partitive phrase 'from/of the Turanian host' specifying which people are meant; classical Persian often uses ز = 'of/from' to indicate source or affiliation.
segments.s197-g6: بکشتی here is used as a 2nd-person past 'you killed' (not the adverbial 'by ship'); تنش را = 'his body' with ـش = 'his' and را the definite/object marker. فگندی is an archaic/poetic past-tense verb meaning 'you hurled/threw' (the provided lemma is dialectal), and براه 'on the road' or 'onto the way' is an adverbial phrase completing the motion verb. The clause sequence is Agent (you) implicit → verb(s) → object with را.
segments.s197-g7: همه مرزها کرد = 'he made/did all the borders' i.e. 'he overran all the frontiers' (کرد is the past of کردن used causatively/performatively). بی‌تار و پود is an idiomatic pair 'without warp and weft' meaning 'utterly ruined/stripped bare'; the negating prefix بی- attaches to تار ('warp'/'string') and is paired with پود ('weft') by و.
segments.s197-g8: همی رفت پیروز = 'he continued/went on victorious' where همی is an archaic/progressive adverb 'still/continually', رفت is past 'went', and پیروز is the predicate adjective 'victorious'. تا کاسه‌رود uses تا as 'as far as' to mark the extent/destination (Kasa-rud is a place name).
segments.s197-g9: بدان here is an old compressed prepositional phrase (به + آن → بدان) meaning “to/at that”; the initial ب is the prefix be-. مرز (object of the locative expression) = “frontier.” لشکر is the subject “the army.” فرود آورید is an archaic/polite past formation of فرود آوردن (literally “to cause to descend / to dismount”); آورید appears here as a past form (Ferdowsi’s older poetic morphology) meaning “brought/caused to descend,” so the clause means “the army alighted at that frontier.”
segments.s197-g10: زمین گشت is a simple verbless/predicative sequence where گشت (past of گشتن) means “became / turned out to be.” زان = از + آن (“from/ because of that” or “by those”) links to خیمه‌ها (“those tents”). ناپدید is a predicate adjective “hidden / unseen / vanished.” Together: “the ground became hidden because of those tents” (i.e. the tents covered/hid the ground).
segments.s197-g11: خبر شد is an impersonal/auxiliary construction meaning “news occurred / it was reported.” بترکان in Ferdowsi’s orthography represents بِ + ترکان = “to/for the Turks” (a compressed prepositional phrase); کز introduces the subordinate content clause (ک + ز = که از/that from) and links to ایران سپاه (“an army from Iran”). Here the clause overall: “news happened to the Turks that an army from Iran…”
segments.s197-g12: سوی = “toward.” کاسه رود is a place name (literally “Kaseh River”); اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase where اندر (= in/into) + آمد (came) yields “came into/approached.” براه here = “on the road / on the march.” The whole segment describes the direction of approach: “was coming toward the Kaseh River on the road.”
segments.s197-g13: ز ترکان = “from the Turks” (ز is the poetic form of از). بیامد is the past “came.” دلیری جوان is an adjectival nominal phrase: دلیری (bravery/valor) qualifies جوان (young man) and is best rendered as “a valorous youth.” Word order (modifier before noun) is normal in Persian poetic style.
segments.s197-g14: This is a string of epithets describing that youth. پلاشان is an epithet meaning someone wearing a coarse/felt cloak (here describing appearance). بیداردل is a compound adjective (بیدار + دل) meaning “wakeful/alert-hearted” (vigilant or watchful in feeling). پهلوان is the head noun “champion/hero.” The sequence is noun phrases in apposition: “a champion, in a felt cloak, watchful‑hearted.”
segments.s197-g15: بیامد = “he came.” که here introduces purpose (که … بنگرد = “so that / in order that he might …”) rather than a simple relative. لشکر is the object of the verb بنگرد; همی is the frequentative/adverbial particle used in classical Persian to mark ongoing or habitual action (here it accompanies the present subjunctive بنگرد used by Ferdowsi to express the intended observation). بنگرد is subjunctive/old present of نگریستن “to look.”
segments.s197-g16: درفش سران را is a direct-object phrase: درفش = “banner/standard,” سران = “the chiefs/leaders,” and را marks the definite object. همی again indicates the continuous/habitual sense with the verb. بشمرد is the (classical) past/subjunctive-like form of شمردن “to count,” here meaning “to count/reckon.” Together: “(he) would count the chiefs’ standards,” i.e. inspecting and counting the banners.
segments.s197-g17: بلشکرگه (army camp) is the noun head; اندر is the preposition 'in/within' and is placed before the phrase it governs; یکی کوه is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain mountain' (یکی as 'a certain'), and بود is the past copula 'was' placed at the end. The whole segment reads literally 'the army-camp was in a certain mountain.'
segments.s197-g18: بلند و بیکسو are coordinated descriptors: بلند 'high/tall' and بیکسو an adverbial/locative meaning 'on one side' or 'to one side'. ز introduces a causa/partitive relation 'from/with' and links to انبوه 'a throng/dense multitude'; بود is again the past copula. Together: 'it was high and on one side (it) was from/with a multitude (i.e. crowded on one side)'.
segments.s197-g19: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival 'seated' describing the subjects; برو here is a poetic/archaic particle derived from 'go' used adverbially to indicate position/proximity (not a literal motion here); گیو و بیژن are the coordinated subjects; بهم 'to one another/together' prepares the reciprocal relation that links to the verb in the next segment. So this segment sets up 'Giv and Bijan, seated together...'.
segments.s197-g20: همی is the iterative/progressive particle 'kept/was continually' placed before the past verb رفت 'went' which here means 'went on/continued' (i.e. continued doing/speaking). هرگونه is an adverbial phrase 'in any way/in every manner'; از introduces the topic 'about/concerning' and is followed by بیش و کم 'more and less' (i.e. great and small matters). The clause means 'they kept on in every manner concerning matters big and small.'
segments.s197-g21: درفش is 'banner/standard' and پلاشان is an adjective here meaning 'tattered/ragged' modifying درفش; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' linking توران سپاه 'the army of Turan' as the source. Word order is descriptive: 'the ragged banner from Turan's army.'
segments.s197-g22: بدیدار is an infinitival/adverbial form meaning 'to meet' or 'for meeting'; ایشان 'them' is the object of that meeting; برآمد is the past verb 'emerged/issued forth' and ز راه 'from the road' gives the origin. The literal order is 'to meet them issued forth from the road' → 'came out of the road to meet them.'
segments.s197-g23: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از دور 'from afar' is an adverbial phrase of distance placed before the direct object; گیو دلاور is the object with appositive epithet 'Giv the valiant'; بدید is the past verb 'saw.' The clause reads 'when (he) saw Giv the valiant from afar.'
segments.s197-g24: This line records two rapid actions: بزد (he struck/struck out) with دست 'hand' and (و) then تیغ ... برکشید 'drew the sword'. از میان 'from the midst' modifies برکشید, and برکشید is used in the sense 'to draw (a weapon)'. The conjunction و links the two coordinated actions (strike with the hand and draw the sword).
segments.s197-g73: بزخم اندرون تیغ شد لخت لخت is a compact poetic clause: بزخم = ب + زخم ‘with/at the wound’ (prepositional phrase marking the location/means), اندرون = ‘inside/within’. تیغ شد is a verbal expression where تیغ (the sword) is the cause/agent and شد (past of شدن) means ‘became/was made’ — so ‘by the sword (the inner parts) were made…’. لخت لخت is a reduplicated adjective ‘lakh(t) lakh(t)’ used adverbially to mean ‘bare, strip by strip’ or ‘piece by piece’ and attaches to شد as its result. Together: ‘(by the sword) the inward parts at the wound became bare, strip by strip.’
segments.s197-g74: ببودند لرزان چو شاخ درخت: ببودند is an archaic/poetic past-form of بودن ‘they were’ (here simply ‘they were’). لرزان is an adjective/predicative participle ‘trembling’ describing the same plural subject. چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’. شاخ درخت is the simile’s image ‘a branch of a tree’ (شاخ = branch, درخت = tree). Word order is Subject–Predicate reduced: ‘they were trembling like a tree’s branch.’
segments.s197-g75: بآب اندرون غرقه شد بارگی: بآب = ب (preposition) + آب ‘with/in water’ indicating the medium; اندرون = ‘inside/within’. غرقه شد is a resultative passive-like phrase ‘became drenched/immersed’. بارگی (foliage/leaf) is the affected noun, here used metaphorically (the ‘foliage’ of helmets, hair, etc.) and is the grammatical subject that ‘became drenched’. Literal structure: ‘The foliage was drenched within with water.’
segments.s197-g76: سرانشان غمی گشت یکبارگی: سرانشان = سران + شان ‘their heads/leaders’ (possessive شان). غمی is a nominal predicate ‘grief/sorrow’ used with گشت (past of گشتن) to mean ‘became sorrow/grieved’. یکبارگی is an adverb ‘suddenly/at once’ modifying the verbal phrase. Word order: topical subject (سرانشان) + predicate (غمی گشت یکبارگی) → ‘their leaders/their heads suddenly became sorrowful.’
segments.s197-g77: عمود گران برکشیدند باز: عمود گران is a compound-like phrase: عمود ‘shaft/pole’ qualified by گران ‘heavy’ (adjective after noun, a common poetic order). برکشیدند is the past plural verb ‘they raised/lifted up’. باز is an adverb ‘again/once more’ modifying the verb. So literally: ‘they raised the heavy shafts again.’
segments.s197-g78: دو شیر سرافراز و دو رزمساز is a coordinated noun phrase naming the raise-ers or the fighters: دو شیر ‘two lions’ (a metaphor for brave men), سرافراز is an epithet ‘proud/lofty/triumphant’; و links to the next noun phrase; دو رزمساز ‘two warriors’ (رزمساز here = fighters/warriors). The phrase lists formidable fighters associated with the previous action of lifting shafts.
segments.s197-g79: چنین تا برآورد بیژن خروش: چنین ‘thus/so’ is an introductory adverb. تا here functions in a temporal/conditional sense close to ‘when/once’ (classical usage often permits ‘when’); برآورد is the past/preterite of برآوردن ‘to bring forth/utter/produce’ used with خروش (roar/cry) as its object: ‘uttered a cry’. بیژن is the subject (Bijan). Together: ‘Thus, when Bijan uttered a cry…’.
segments.s197-g80: عمودگران برنهاده بدوش: عمودگران is the plural agent-noun ‘shaft-bearers’ or ‘those with the poles’. برنهاده is a perfect/participial form of برنهادن ‘placed/put’ (here used as a past action: ‘having placed/they placed’). بدوش is a fused form of به دوش ‘on (their) shoulder(s)’ indicating the location where the poles were set. The clause means: ‘the shaft-bearers set/placed (them) on their shoulders.’
segments.s197-g97: برفتند is the 3rd‑person plural past of رفتن with the prefix بر‑ meaning ‘departed/left’. The adverbial phrase با شادمانی (با + noun) follows the verb and expresses manner ‘with rejoicing’. ز جای is a poetic short form of از جای and marks the origin/place ‘from the place’. Word order here is verb — manner phrase — source.
segments.s197-g98: نهادند is the 3rd‑person plural past of نهادن ‘to place/set’. سر is the direct object ‘head’; سوی + noun gives the direction ‘toward’. پرده‌سرای is a compound meaning the curtained hall/stage; the whole phrase means they ‘set/turned their heads toward the curtain‑hall’. Note the verb precedes its object and the directional phrase.
segments.s197-g99: بیاورد is a past (classical) 3rd‑singular form meaning ‘he brought’ (bi‑ prefix + آورد). پیش سپهبد is a prepositional phrase ‘before/in the presence of the spahbed’. سرش is the noun سر ‘head’ plus the possessive enclitic ‑ش ‘his/its’; here it functions as the direct object of بیاورد. The indirect object (پیش سپهبد) sits between verb and object.
segments.s197-g100: همان is the demonstrative ‘that/the same’. اسپ is an older word for ‘steed/horse’. با introduces an accompanying instrument/attire ‘with’. جوشن is ‘cuirass/armor’; و links items; مغفرش is مغفر ‘covering/veil’ plus the possessive suffix ‑ش ‘its’, so ‘and its covering’. The whole string apposes or enumerates what was brought (the steed, equipped with armor and its covering).
segments.s197-g101: چنان ... شد is a standard construction meaning ‘became so/so much’. زان is a contracted poetic form of از + آن ‘from/at that’; سخن is ‘speech/words’; پهلوان is the subject ‘the champion/hero’. Together: ‘the champion became so happy at that speech.’
segments.s197-g102: که introduces a relative/clause of result ‘that/which’. گفتی here functions as ‘you said’ (classical 2nd‑person past), but the supplied lemma senses list did not include the verbal sense, so its sense is marked below as unavailable. برافشاند is a verb meaning ‘to cast forth/scatter’; خواهد is the auxiliary ‘will’ placed after the verb (inverted order typical in poetry) to form a future; روان is the object ‘soul/spirit’. Literally: ‘that (you said) will cast his soul forth.’
segments.s197-g103: بدو گفت is literally ‘to him he said’ (بدو = to him, prepositional pronoun; گفت is ‘said’). کای is a vocative particle ‘O!’. پور means ‘son’ or ‘young man’; پشت سپاه (پشت = support/protection here) is a laudatory epithet ‘backbone/support of the army’. The sequence is a vocative phrase addressing the addressee with honorifics.
segments.s197-g104: سر here is used figuratively ‘head/chief’ (not anatomical). It governs the following nouns: نامداران ‘the famed/renowned ones’ and, joined by و, دیهیم ‘the rural/peasant folk’. شاه at the end is vocative/appellative ‘O king’ or ‘king’ addressing him. The phrase as a whole lists titles: ‘chief of the renowned and of the rural people, O king.’
segments.s197-g49: همی + simple verb is a classical Persian habitual/continuous marker: همی خورد = 'was eating / kept eating'. The clause then links with و 'and' to a second nominal cluster: اسپش چران = اسپ + possessive -ش 'his horse' followed by چران as a verbal/adjectival participle 'grazing' (the horse is grazing). چمان here works as 'such/so' connecting to the following segment.
segments.s197-g50: پلاشان is the proper name (subject) here; نشسته is a perfective/past participle used predicatively 'was seated / sat'. به introduces the accompanying prepositional phrase 'on/at' (here 'on his arm'), with بازو 'arm' as the local noun and کمان 'bow' specifying what is on the arm. The whole phrase means 'Plashan sat, a bow on his arm.'
segments.s197-g51: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. اسپش 'his steed' (possessive -ش) is the subject; ز دور is an adverbial 'from a distance' modifying the perception verb; اسپ بیژن is 'Bijan's horse' (object of seeing). بدید is the past form 'saw'. Word order is Subject — adverbial — object — verb, typical in poetry.
segments.s197-g52: خروشی برآورد is a light-verb construction: برآورد (here 'raised, produced') takes خروشی 'a cry/roar' as its object, meaning 'it uttered a cry.' The conjunction و links a second action: اندر دمید 'and breathed/blowed in' — in context of a horse this means it snorted/blew out air. اندر is the locative/particle 'in/into' and دمید is past 'blew/breathed.'
segments.s197-g53: پلاشان is the subject (proper name); بدانست is a compound past 'knew/perceived that' (بـ + دانست). The following phrase کامد سوار is a nominal + verb where کامد (archaic present/past form) means 'was coming' or 'came' and سوار 'a rider' is the logical subject of that subordinate clause: 'Plashan understood that a rider was coming.'
segments.s197-g54: بیامد is the past 'came' (reiterating arrival). بسیچیده is an adjective/participle 'well‑arrayed / abundantly gathered' modifying the implied force; کارزار 'battle' (here 'for battle' / 'to battle') completes the idea: 'came, well‑arrayed for battle.' The adjective precedes the noun as is common in epic diction.
segments.s197-g55: یکی here functions as 'someone' (an indefinite subject). بانگ برزد is an idiom meaning 'raised a cry' — برزد is an archaic perfective used with بانگ to indicate producing a sound. به بیژن 'to Bijan' marks the addressee, and بلند 'loud' qualifies the manner of the cry.
segments.s197-g56: The clause begins with منم 'I am / it is I' followed by گفت 'said' (classical past 'he said'); what follows are proper names (شیراوژن و دیوبند) uttered as the content of the speech. The sequence is the narrator's brief report of the spoken words: 'I am ... he said (the names)'.
segments.s197-g33: به بیژن is a prepositional phrase marking the indirect object ('to Bijan'). چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'said thus/so'; in the poem the speaker's name and epithet (گیو دلیر) follow the verb for poetic word order, so گیو is the subject and دلیر is a postpositive adjective 'the brave.'
segments.s197-g34: که introduces the reported content clause ('that' / introducing the words said). مشتاب is an imperative 'hasten/be quick' (2nd singular). در چنگ این نره شیر is a locative/prepositional phrase 'into/in the clutch/claws of this male lion' where چنگ means 'claw/grip', نره specifies 'male (animal)'.
segments.s197-g35: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'one must not / you should not'; that is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. با او 'with him' marks the adversary. نتابی here is from تابیدن in the sense 'to stand/withstand, endure/contend' (2nd person singular), and بجنگ is the verbal complement 'to fight' (infinitive/subjunctive construction).
segments.s197-g36: کنی is the 2nd-person subjunctive/present of کردن used to complete the periphrastic verb phrase بجنگ کنی 'if you do fight'. روز is a temporal noun 'that day'; بر من is a prepositional phrase 'upon me / for me'; برین is the poetic/orthographic form of 'این' (meaning 'this' / 'that') qualifying جنگ 'battle'; تنگ is a predicative adjective 'narrow/close/difficult' describing the battle's character — word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s197-g37: پلاشان is a plural noun (here 'cloaks' or 'pelts/robes') serving as the subject. چو شیر است is a simile 'is like a lion' and در مرغزار is a locative 'in the meadow' — overall a poetic image comparing the garment/appearance to a lion in a field.
segments.s197-g38: جز از ... نجوید شکار is a limiting construction 'seeks prey from none except ...' — جز از means 'except/from aside'. مرد جنگی is a compound 'a warlike man / a valiant man' (مرد here carries the sense 'hero/valiant man'), and نجوید is the archaic/poetic 3rd-person present 'seeks' (so the sense is 'does not seek except from such a one'), with شکار 'prey' as the object.
segments.s197-g39: بدو 'to him' is an enclitic/prepositional phrase marking the indirect object of گفت 'said'. بیژن is the speaker (subject). م + را together form the accusative 'مرا' ('me'); the data here is split into two tokens, so م is the pronoun portion and را is the definite-object marker — together 'مرا' = 'me'. زین سخن is 'by/with this speech' or 'with these words' (زین is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'this' or 'by this'), so the whole segment supplies the quoted content's object (Bijan asking not to be shamed by these words).
segments.s197-g40: به پیش جهاندار is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the sovereign' (جهاندار = ruler/sovereign). ننگی مکن is a negative imperative 'do not cause disgrace / do not disgrace' (ننگی = 'disgrace', مکن = 'do not'). Together it is the plea/command 'do not disgrace me before the king.'
segments.s197-g89: دل گیو بد is a nominal phrase meaning “Giv’s heart was ill/afflicted”: دل (heart) precedes the possessor گیو, and بد functions as a predicative adjective ‘sick/ill/troubled.’ زان سخن پر ز درد is a prepositional phrase 'from that speech, full of pain' where زان = از + آن 'from that', پر ز = 'full of', and درد names the emotional pain causing the state.
segments.s197-g90: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason or the thought content; چون here is interrogative/conditional 'how / if', and گردد is the classical third-person present-subjunctive (or optative-flavored) of 'to become' used for a hypothetical situation. آن باد روز نبرد is a poetic sequence where باد works as an optative/wish particle ('may it be') and روز نبرد = 'a day of battle.' Together: '…thinking how it would be if that day were to become a day of battle.'
segments.s197-g91: خروشان و جوشان are two linked participial/adjectival words 'roaring and seething' describing mood or movement; بدان is the fused prepositional phrase به + آن 'at/to that' (here 'at that'), and دیده‌گاه is the place-word 'watch‑point / lookout.' The whole phrase describes people being restless at that vantage.
segments.s197-g92: که تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/extent: که تا = 'so that / until', with تا functioning as 'until' or 'in order to see.' کی آید ز راه is the core question-word construction 'when will (he) come from the road' (کی = when, آید = comes). گرد here is a poetic/local particle (literary 'around/about' or linking element) tying بیژن to the clause; overall: '…eager at the lookout to see when Bijan will come from the road.'
segments.s197-g93: همی آمد is a literary/perfective narrative phrase where همی intensifies or indicates immediacy/continuity and آمد is 'came.' از راه is the prepositional phrase 'from the road,' and پور جوان is the nominal subject 'the young son / young man' (پور = son). Word order here is typical of epic Persian: adverb + verb + prepositional phrase + subject.
segments.s197-g94: This is a paratactic list 'سر و جوشن و اسپ' = 'head (i.e. helmet/head), cuirass, and steed' joined by و. آن پهلوان is a postnominal tag 'that hero' identifying whose equipment these are; the demonstrative آن picks out the named champion.
segments.s197-g95: بیاورد is the past tense 'he brought' (narrative), و links the two actions, بنهاد is an archaic past tense ‘he put/laid/placed,’ and پیش پدر is the prepositional phrase 'before his father / in his father's presence.' The implied subject is the same for both verbs.
segments.s197-g96: بدو = به + او 'to him,' گفت is the narrative past 'said' introducing direct speech, and پیروز باش‌ای پسر is the quoted imperative: پیروز 'victorious' with باش (imperative 'be!') plus the vocative ای پسر 'O son.' The clause is 'he said to him: “Be victorious, O son.”'
segments.s197-g65: همی is an older/dialectal particle here meaning 'continually' or 'kept on' and sets a habitual/ongoing scene. The nouns دود و خاکستر و خون form a coordinated list joined by و; the final word خوری is from خوردن used in the sense 'befall/happen' (an impersonal or intransitive result), so the verb of the clause comes at the end and takes the whole listing as its theme: 'smoke and ash and blood fell/befell (continually)'. This word order (series of nouns + verbal form at the end) is typical in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s197-g66: گه آمد که is a fixed temporal phrase: گه آمد = 'there would come times / sometimes it happened that'; که introduces the subordinate clause. لشکر is the subject 'army/host', بهامون is the prepositional/directional phrase 'toward us', and بری is an adjective 'wild/untamed' modifying لشکر (adjectives often follow the noun in poetry). The verb 'آمد' is simple past/habitual here ('came' / 'would come'), giving the sense 'an untamed army would come against us'.
segments.s197-g67: پلاشان here functions as the subject (literally 'their rags/coverings' in one sense, but in context it names the lamentations/ailing condition — grammatically it's a nominative head). بپاسخ is the older/preverbal formative of the verb 'to answer' and together with نکرد (negated past of کردن used as auxiliary) forms the negative past 'did not answer' (بپاسخ نکرد = 'gave no answer'). ایچ precedes یاد and is a strong negator 'not at all / nothing', so ایچ یاد = 'not a trace of memory / no recollection'. Note the stacked negation/nominal phrase: subject + negative verb + emphatic negator + noun.
segments.s197-g68: برانگیخت is a past tense transitive verb 'roused / caused to stir'. آن پیل‌تن را is the object phrase with را marking definiteness/object; classical Persian permits the object (with را) to appear after or before the verb for poetic effect. چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' modifying the manner in which the stirring (or lack of it) occurred. Grammatically, یاد (previous segment) is what 'did (not) arouse'—here the clause says 'not a trace of memory roused that elephant-bodied one, like the wind.'
segments.s197-g69: سواران is the plural subject 'horsemen/riders'. بنیزه is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with spears' (in older Persian the preposition may be absent and the noun follows the subject to indicate instrument). برآویختند is past plural 'they leapt up / sprang upon' (بر- prefix + آویختند), so the segment describes the riders' sudden movement armed with spears.
segments.s197-g70: یکی is the numeral/indefinite 'one' (here introducing a single mass/instance), followed by گرد 'dust' (sense 'dust' rather than 'around') and the adjective تیره 'dark' placed after the noun (adjectives often follow nouns in poetry). برانگیختند is the past plural verb 'they raised/sent up' (here used of a cloud rising). So the sequence literally reads 'they raised one dark dust-cloud.' Word order: numeral + noun + postposed adjective + final verb.
segments.s197-g71: سنانهای (the plural form of 'سنان', 'spear-point' / 'spear') combined with نیزه 'spear' gives a reiterating/poetic compound 'the (points of) spears'. بهم here means 'against one another' / 'together', and برشکست is past tense 'broke / were shattered'. The verb follows noun phrases as usual, giving 'the spear-points of the spears shattered together.'
segments.s197-g72: یلان is the subject 'the heroes/champions'. سوی شمشیر is a directional phrase 'toward the sword(s)' and بردند is the past plural 'they took / they carried'; دست ('hand') at the end completes the idiom دست بردن سوی شمشیر = 'to put one's hand to the sword', i.e. 'to draw a sword'. Classical Persian often arranges the components of such idioms in this order for emphasis: subject + direction + object-verb + accessory noun.
segments.s197-g81: بزد is a simple past verb ('he struck'). بر میان پلاشان گرد is a prepositional phrase: بر میان = 'on/upon the middle (of)', پلاشان = 'their coarse cloaks' (plāshān with the third‑person plural possessive implied by context), and گرد here is an adverb 'around/about'. Word order is verb → prepositional object (typical in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s197-g82: همه مهرهٔ پشت is a noun phrase 'all the vertebrae of the back' (همه = 'all', مهره = 'vertebra', پشت = 'back'). بشکست is past tense 'broke' (third‑person), and خرد functions as an adjective/adverbial complement 'into small pieces' (i.e. 'broke small'). The verb follows the full object phrase, a common order in Ferdowsi.
segments.s197-g83: ز بالای اسپ اندر آمد is a classical locative/verb sequence: ز بالای اسپ = 'from atop the horse' (ز = 'from', بالای = 'above/atop', اسپ = 'horse'), and اندر آمد = 'came down / alit' (اندر as a prepositional element + آمد 'came'). تنش is 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش 'his').
segments.s197-g84: نگون شد is a past‑state expression 'was overthrown / was thrown down' (نگون = 'overthrown', شد = 'became/was'). بر introduces the surface 'upon/onto' and governs the following coordinated direct objects مغفر و جوشنش ('his maghfar/covering and his cuirass'); و are simple coordinators. Note جوشنش contains the possessive -ش 'his'.
segments.s197-g85: فرود آمد = 'fell/came down' (a compound nominal/verb 'descent/came'), introduced by از باره بیژن = 'from the blow/occasion of Bijan' (باره here = 'stroke/instance [of striking]'). چو گرد is 'when/so that (it) turned' — چو = 'when/as', گرد functioning as a verbal element 'to turn/roll' (classical usage).
segments.s197-g86: سرِ مردِ جنگی is the direct object 'the head of the war‑man/warrior' (سر + construct), ز تن = 'from the body' (ز = 'from', تن = 'body'), and دور کرد is a verbal phrase 'caused to be removed / turned away' (دور = 'away/removed', کرد = past of کردن used here with a causative/active force). The verb applies to the preceding object, so the head was made to be away from the body.
segments.s197-g87: This is a coordinated noun group listing the items taken: سلیح و سر و اسپ = 'weapon and head and horse', followed by آن نامجوی 'that Namjuy' which identifies whose these items are (the demonstrative + personal name). The order places the possessed items before the possessor in poetic style.
segments.s197-g88: بیاورد is a past tense verb 'he brought' (perfective). و سوی پدر کرد روی is an idiomatic verb phrase: سوی پدر = 'toward his father' (سوی = 'toward', پدر = 'father') and کرد روی literally 'made [his] face' = 'turned his face' (کرد + روی). In classical Persian 'کرد روی' means 'turned toward / faced'.
segments.s197-g57: بگو is the imperative 'say'; آشکارا is an adverb 'plainly/openly' placed after the verb for emphasis. که here is a complementizer introducing the embedded question, and نام تو چیست is an interrogative clause with the copula -ست (چیست) at the end: 'what is your name?'.
segments.s197-g58: The initial که gives a reason ('for' / 'because'). اختر is the subject 'the star'. همی here is an archaic/emphatic prefix meaning roughly 'still/indeed' (intensifying the action). بر is the preposition 'upon/over' with the pronominal تو as its object. خواهد is the auxiliary 'will' (future), and گریست is the verb 'to weep' (old/perfective form used here with خواهد to mean 'will weep').
segments.s197-g59: دلاور is the nominative noun 'the valiant one/hero' serving as subject; بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (be + pronoun). گفت is the past tense 'said' introducing direct speech, and من بیژنم is the speaker's self-identification: من (I) + بیژنم where the name takes the 1st-person ending -م yielding 'I am Bijan' (copula is implied).
segments.s197-g60: This short clause strings descriptive elements: برزم functions as an older/poetic locus word (here roughly 'in my force / in my camp' or a bound form with 1st-person reference), اندرون is the locative 'within/inside', and پیل is the noun 'elephant' (the thing inside). و is the coordinating 'and', and رویین‌تنم is a predicative/adjectival compound 'iron-bodied' with the 1st-person -م ('I am iron-bodied' or 'my body is iron'). The clause thus lists what is inside and gives the speaker's armored description.
segments.s197-g61: نیا here acts as an exclamatory/vocative particle (an older poetic usage akin to 'lo' or 'behold'); شیر جنگی is a compound 'war-lion' (a figurative epithet for a fierce warrior); پدر گیو is an appositive 'father of Giv' (identifying who the war-lion is); گرد is used adverbially/verbally as 'around/has come around' (classical usage meaning 'is about/has turned up').
segments.s197-g62: هم‌اکنون is the temporal adverb 'right now' or 'at once'. ببینی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive form of 'to see' used here with a near-future sense 'you will see'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', من is the first-person pronoun 'me', and دستبرد is the noun 'raid/seizure/attack' — together 'you will see my attack'.
segments.s197-g63: This is a sequence of time/condition phrases: بروز here means 'on the day/occasion (of)', بلا is 'calamity/affliction', در دم is a fixed phrase 'at the moment/instant' (دم = moment), and کارزار is 'battle/field of battle'. Together they set the temporal frame: 'on the day of calamity, at the moment of battle.'
segments.s197-g64: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. بر کوه is the locative phrase 'on the mountain' (بر + object). چون is the simile marker 'like/as', گرگ is 'wolf', مردار here is adjectival/attributive 'carrion (seeking)' or 'of carrion' (i.e., a carrion-hunting wolf), and خواه is the auxiliary 'will' (future), giving the clause a future/predictive force: 'you will be on the mountain like a carrion-seeking wolf.'

sentences.s197-s53: May a merry, proud she‑goat always be far from you, you wicked evildoer.
sentences.s197-s13: Thus he said, “A lion in a tattered cloak has come — a certain renowned, brave horseman.”
sentences.s197-s14: May I be accursed if I cut off his head from his body; if I bind him, I will bring him to this assembly.
sentences.s197-s15: Bijan said to him, 'If the king grants me a robe of honour in this battle...'
sentences.s197-s16: By my command I must gird my waist; I must do battle with the tattered, quarrelsome ones.
sentences.s197-s21: Give me Siavash's weapon for fighting; then stand ready and watch the leopard's quarry.
sentences.s197-s22: Giv the brave gave him that armor; Bijan then fastened the armor's knot.
sentences.s197-s23: Suddenly the swift one sprang up; he strode toward us, spear in hand.
sentences.s197-s24: On his cloak he had laid a gazelle; its kebab was spread over the fire.
sentences.s197-s1: They tarried three days inside the leather (tents); on the fourth a blast rose from the trumpet.
sentences.s197-s2: The host took up arms and struck the ney and the drum; the land from mountain to mountain became ebony-black.
sentences.s197-s3: Whomever of the Turanian host you saw, you killed and flung his body onto the road.
sentences.s197-s4: He laid all the frontiers waste, leaving them without warp and weft; he kept going victorious as far as Kasa-rud.
sentences.s197-s5: At that frontier the army descended; the ground was hidden by those tents.
sentences.s197-s6: News reached the Turks that an army from Iran was coming toward the Kaseh River on the road.
sentences.s197-s7: From the Turks came a valorous youth — a champion in a felt cloak, watchful‑hearted.
sentences.s197-s8: He came so that he might look upon the army, counting the chiefs’ standards.
sentences.s197-s9: The army camp stood on a certain high mountain, and on one side it was crowded with a throng.
sentences.s197-s10: Giv and Bijan, seated together, kept going on (their talk) in every manner, about matters great and small.
sentences.s197-s11: The Turanian army's ragged standard appeared from the road to meet them.
sentences.s197-s12: When (he) saw the valiant Giv from afar, he struck (with his) hand and drew his sword from its midst.
sentences.s197-s37: By the sword the inner parts through the wounds were laid bare, strip by strip; they trembled like a tree's branch.
sentences.s197-s38: Their foliage was soaked within with water; their chiefs were suddenly filled with grief.
sentences.s197-s39: They once more lifted up heavy shafts; two proud lions and two warriors.
sentences.s197-s40: Thus, when Bijan raised a cry, the shaft-bearers placed (the poles) upon their shoulders.
sentences.s197-s49: They left the place rejoicing and turned their heads toward the curtain‑hall.
sentences.s197-s50: He brought before the spahbed his head — that very steed, with its cuirass and its covering.
sentences.s197-s51: The champion became so glad at that speech that, as you said, it would send his spirit flying.
sentences.s197-s52: He said to him, 'O son, support of the army, chief of the renowned and king of the countryside!'
sentences.s197-s25: He kept eating, while his horse grazed; Plashan sat with a bow upon his arm.
sentences.s197-s26: When his steed from afar saw Bijan's horse, it raised a cry and snorted.
sentences.s197-s27: Plashan perceived that a rider was coming, come arrayed for battle.
sentences.s197-s28: Someone raised a cry loud to Bijan; "I — Shirāvazhan — and Dīvband," he said.
sentences.s197-s17: Giv the brave said thus to Bijan: "Hurry into the claws of this male lion."
sentences.s197-s18: You must not engage him in combat; if you do fight him, that day this battle will be tight for me.
sentences.s197-s19: Their cloaks (or pelts) are like a lion in the meadow; they seek prey from none but a warlike man.
sentences.s197-s20: Bijan said to him, "Do not disgrace me with these words before the sovereign."
sentences.s197-s45: Giv's heart was distressed by that speech, full of pain, wondering how it would be if that day should become a day of battle.
sentences.s197-s46: They (or those present) were roaring and seething at that lookout, eager to see when Bijan would come from the road.
sentences.s197-s47: From the road there came the young son — that hero — with his head, cuirass, and horse.
sentences.s197-s48: He brought them and placed them before his father; to him he said, 'Be victorious, O son!'
sentences.s197-s33: There was constant smoke and ash and blood; sometimes an untamed host came against us.
sentences.s197-s34: Their cries received no reply; not a trace of memory stirred that elephant-bodied man like the wind.
sentences.s197-s35: The horsemen leapt up with spears; they raised a dark cloud of dust.
sentences.s197-s36: The spear-points shattered together; the heroes put their hands to their swords.
sentences.s197-s41: He struck at the middle of their cloaks, and all the vertebrae of the back were broken into small pieces.
sentences.s197-s42: His body came down from atop the horse; he was overturned upon his covering and his cuirass.
sentences.s197-s43: Bijan's blow fell down, so that the head of the war‑man was turned away from his body.
sentences.s197-s44: That Namjuy brought his weapon and head and horse, and turned his face toward his father.
sentences.s197-s29: Say plainly what your name is, for the star will weep over you.
sentences.s197-s30: The valiant one said to him, 'I am Bijan; inside (my) force are elephants, and I am iron-bodied.'
sentences.s197-s31: Lo, a war-lion — Giv's father — has come round; even now you shall see my assault.
sentences.s197-s32: On the day of calamity, at the moment of battle, you will be on the mountain like a carrion-seeking wolf.
